name: Nox
description: Nox, the Roman goddess of night, is a primordial deity, born of Chaos itself. She is the personification of darkness and shadow, a powerful and enigmatic figure who predates even many of the Olympian gods. Nox is the mother of numerous offspring, often associated with darkness, dreams, and death, including Somnus (Sleep), Mors (Death), and the Moirae (Fates). Her realm is the night sky, a vast and mysterious expanse where she reigns supreme. While not actively worshipped in temples like other deities, Nox was acknowledged and respected for her immense power and influence. She is often depicted veiled and cloaked, driving a chariot drawn by black horses across the heavens, ushering in the darkness that blankets the world. Her presence is both feared and necessary, as night provides rest and respite, but also conceals dangers and secrets. Nox's influence extends beyond mere darkness; she represents the unknown, the hidden aspects of existence, and the power of the unseen. She is a reminder that even in the brightest light, shadows always exist, and that darkness holds its own unique power and mysteries. Her role is crucial in maintaining the balance between day and night, light and darkness, life and death, ensuring the cyclical nature of existence. She is the embodiment of the primal fear and awe that humans feel when confronted with the unknown and the infinite.
subtitle: Goddess of Night and Darkness

keyMyths.title: Nox and Hypnos
keyMyths.description: Nox hid Hypnos (Sleep) from Zeus, protecting him from the king of the gods' wrath after Hypnos helped the Trojans.

keyMyths.title: Nox's Children
keyMyths.description: Nox, born of Chaos, birthed numerous children, many representing negative aspects like death (Mors) and strife (Eris), reflecting the darkness she embodies.

keyMyths.title: Nox and the Chariot
keyMyths.description: Nox drives a chariot pulled by black horses across the sky, bringing the night and darkness after day.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A genealogical account of the gods, including the origins of Nox and her children.
sources.text: A genealogical account of the gods, including the origins of Nox and her children.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Mentions Nox's role in protecting Hypnos from Zeus.
